label: How did grandfather come to Hong Kong?
visitor_question: Tell us about your grandfather's story.
question_zh: 請告訴我們你祖父的故事。
answer_text_en: My grandfather—yes. So my father's grandfather, my father's father, he was an orphan when he was very young. His parents sent him to Shanghai to be apprenticed, to be apprenticed. Later on, he went to Singapore with those people. After he arrived in Singapore, well, because Japan invaded China and Southeast Asia, Singapore was occupied by the Japanese. So around 1945, or '40—well, around '45—they couldn't stay in Singapore anymore. They had to go back to Hong Kong. My grandfather told me they took a ship from Singapore, but they stayed in Hong Kong. After they got to Hong Kong, at that time, China was being invaded by Japan. There was no safety in China, no safety. Everything was being destroyed. My grandfather told me they walked through the countryside for three months before they could get down. Then the war ended, temporarily ended in 1945. At that time—this was before I was born—yes, before I was born. I was born in 1946. In 1949, China was established as a communist state. My father's family used to work in Shanghai. He went to work in a Shanghai factory, and later went to Wenzhou. When I was young, I also went to work in a Wenzhou factory. Some people went to Hong Kong and called for my grandfather to come to Hong Kong. My grandfather first took a car from Wenzhou to Macau, and from Macau people helped arrange passage, and then he went to Hong Kong.

answer_text_en:
No, yes—he took a car to Macau, and then from Macau to Hong Kong. That's how it was back then. He went to Hong Kong, and we were left behind. Then we said, "Your grandmother applied to the government, and we said we wanted to go to Hong Kong, but we had to wait two or three years—no, we couldn't go yet."

So later, why was it that in 1960 he could go? Do you know about Chinese history? In 1960, it was a time of great chaos—the Great Famine. They had this Great Famine. At that time, Mao Zedong said in fifteen years they would be better than America. And then, uh, Beijing, and so on. And then that campaign failed. So families were saying there's no food, there's not enough. So at that time in our commune, nobody was allowed to cook food in their own homes. Everyone had to eat in the communal dining hall because you had no food of your own. Only everyone together. When there was no rice, we'd eat cabbage—sweet pork with cabbage. Dried fruit, but sometimes when it rained we'd eat it anyway. Sometimes we'd make cakes out of grass, and the water would drain away.

We came from Luohu to Hong Kong. We came from Sichuan to Hong Kong. Your father was in Hong Kong, so you didn't see him for many years—from when you were small until you were fifteen, you didn't see him.

No, no, no, it wasn't like that. I think it was only a few years, maybe five years. Before that they were in Hong Kong, but they were usually just doing odd jobs, nothing agricultural. But you also worked in agriculture, he did too.

Yes, but some things were like this. We were like that. We were very lucky to come to Hong Kong. But after we came to Hong Kong, things were even harder.

At that time in Hong Kong, why this international name, the popular name in Hong Kong was "professionals." Because from 1960 to 1962, they were called the "big fugitives." Because most of them came from Guangdong, fleeing from Guangdong to Hong Kong, at that time. But they spoke the same language, you didn't have—yes, yes, that's right. Then they went to school, no English. You had to learn English, and then you learned English.

Every day he had... back then, he was doing... he drove trucks. There were no machines back then, we had trucks. Now you have all these electronic tools. Back then he had to use, like, a big gong or something—you needed two people, working with your hands, your hands. Yes, that's how it started back then. So he worked from morning until, well, morning until maybe eight or nine o'clock or so. So we didn't see him very much. Only occasionally, maybe during the big holidays—like the Mid-Autumn Festival, Chinese New Year, and in May we had the Dragon Boat Festival too. During those festival times, we would go out together. There was a very famous family back then—the Tiger family in Hong Kong. But normally, there wasn't much time to go out with him. Later, when we were in Hong Kong, and then when we went back, we had more time to go out together. We could go back sometimes and spend time together.

Later, when you... after summer, I think... where was it, at the factory where he worked? How did he start his own factory? Did he start his own factory? Yes, yes, yes. First he worked for other people, then he started his own factory. That's how he got started. But his factory was... back then in Hong Kong, you couldn't have a real factory space. You could only, like, use an apartment or a room in someone's apartment—a backroom or whatever—to run the factory from there. So his craftsmanship was very good. At that time he had, I think, about ten or so people working with him. Some of them later went off to start their own backroom factories. One of them went to, what was it, Marlon? No, New York. One time we actually drove out with Uncle Alice to see him there. Now we know someone we see all the time in New York—his name is Chan, we call him Uncle Bao. Uncle Alice often says that Bao has an unmarried daughter and keeps trying to introduce her to you. He's a doctor now, working in Seattle. That's how I know him—at Herman's wedding. Yeah, yeah, yeah. visitor_question: How large were the classes back then?
question_zh: 那時候班級有多大？
answer_text_en:
# Translation

Around 15 or so, his school was in Hong Kong. Back then there were a lot of students but not enough schools, so we had the morning shift and afternoon shift system—have you heard of that? Morning shift, afternoon shift. They did it for profit, you see. So the morning shift would be from 8 o'clock to maybe 1 o'clock, and another class would be the same form level. Some students went in the morning, some in the afternoon. Then there was another session from 2 o'clock to maybe year 6 or year 7. Morning shift, afternoon shift. That's how it was back then because there simply weren't enough schools. Then the next year he switched to another school, hoping it would be better. It wasn't much better. The third school was better, so he went there from form 3 to form 5. Later on there was a middle school exam. Your dad passed, but your mom didn't pass. So they had different paths. Your mom didn't pass, so she learned typing and got a job. 

Dad, what was your high school called? Lutheran Middle School. Lutheran Middle School. It's still there on Waterloo Road. We stayed at a hotel that time near Waterloo Road. There's also Nayten Road. Waterloo Road is actually the original street in that area, near Nayten Road. Waterloo Road stretches all the way to Kowloon Town. Kowloon Town was quite famous with lots of residents, but that was the original stretch.

We lived near the Chinese University—New Asia College. New Asia College is where we stayed, walking distance away. On Heng Hang Street. Then we moved to a new apartment, 6th floor, with an elevator. This was in Hong Kong, right? Yes. We lived only about two blocks away. So basically we were very familiar with Hong Kong. Your mom was also in Hong Kong, though a bit further away. She had it better because they lived in Galman housing, so they had better income. So you lived near CUHK? Right. What was it like those first couple years? Well, the first couple years we were quite familiar with the area, walking distance. Then from... the first couple years we were in a temporary shanty area in To Kwa Wan. Then we moved to Sha Tin. The second year in Sha Tin. But Sha Tin had no toilets, so we had to take the train to get there. Take the train. Very far, right? Sha Tin was new land back then. Oh, it was newly developed land. Very remote.

What was your field of study? Actually, I studied business back then. Business. I tried many business ventures. So it inspired me to start my own business, but it wasn't successful. What was your business? It was... wasn't it part of the factory? No, it was separate. The factory we were involved with later was sales. Grandfather's company was called Tetley—Petaling sales. Grandfather's factory was called Tetley Furniture Company.

That's an English name, right? Tetley, T-A-K, T-A-K, L-E-E, Furniture Company. Tetley—the meaning of the English characters is "Get Profit." "Tet" is "Get," "Lea" is "Profit." Ah, "Mai" is different—"Dragging Wood." So you were working in a shop?

Yes.

But you only sold Tetley Furniture?

No, no, no...

label: Did you sell other things too?
visitor_question: How did the furniture business actually work?
question_zh: 家具生意實際上是如何運作的？
answer_text_en:
I don't sell that many things, because Grandfather had high standards. He didn't want to produce too much, so we had to... otherwise it wouldn't be enough. Grandfather, because he had a high-quality factory, that meant the prices were very high. Not higher quality than other people's, so you have the wood sourcing—that comes with multiple resources. Yes, you know, back when our Furniture Company was starting, we spent 3,500 HKD per month. Every year I'd ask them to reduce the price, and they'd say okay, reduce 10%. But then at that time they asked, why did you buy that property, buy that storefront? Back then the rent was 3,500 HKD, but that property was... 80,000 HKD. But we didn't have the money to buy it.

And there's another interesting thing—he always opposed buying a house. He always opposed it. When we were starting out, we couldn't buy that property. And another thing is, when we started, not many people had money. Everybody had no money. So if I listened to someone buy that property, wow, now they're doing well! I thought maybe it's worth 100 million HKD now. Do you understand?

You know, back then Uncle Bill and them, they bought a place in Sai Kung. Have you been there? Back then he bought something for 900 HKD. But with that same money, if he wanted to buy on Prince Edward Road—Prince Edward Road... if he went to buy two houses on Prince Edward Road... I'm not sure if you've been to Chen Si-lung's house. Later, the prices... some 400 HKD, then 900 HKD... I just felt that maybe seven or eight years later, you could buy something for... 70,000 HKD? With 400 HKD you could get 70,000? So if you don't buy a house in Hong Kong, you lose out. Hong Kong is so crazy about it. The property values there are amazing. Later, someone brought back a contract. Uncle Alice told you—later there was a cat, back when prices were high. He said someone bought in Sai Kung, brought back a contract. Someone offered 60,000 HKD. But Uncle Bill wouldn't buy. He refused to buy. He made his own choice, just rented. He wouldn't buy. Why? Why did it become like that? He also didn't want to buy. He refused. Because he also refused to buy. He refused, he refused. It seems expensive, right? Expensive, expensive. He'd sell it... turns out this cunning one sold it. He made me pay for it myself, so my mother often complains—I gave him so much money, he didn't complain, he works very hard now, but he has no plan, that's just how it is. We got back to Dragonwood Furniture...

question: 你說它沒有成功,你覺得它有多久?
answer_text: Dragonwood Furniture在1974年開始, 爸爸從大學畢業, 因為Dragonwood Furniture的生意不錯, 爸爸參加了香港交通部發展委員會的交通任務, 在19…… 我記得了, 1975年, 出了任務, 有去洛杉磯, 去紐約,邁阿密,和潘拿馬,四個城市, 那時候就推銷我們的產品, 但沒有成功, 後來, 我還有一個高中同學, 他在洛杉磯讀書, 所以他在那裡,爸爸就在洛杉磯買了一間房子。 這就是為什麼我來到華為, 第一次來是在1975年。
label: How long did the business last?
visitor_question: How did the idea of coming to Hawaii come about?
question_zh: 你是如何想到來夏威夷的？
answer_text_en: Dragonwood Furniture started in 1974. After my father graduated from university, because the Dragonwood Furniture business was doing well, he participated in a trade mission with Hong Kong's transportation development committee. I remember now—it was 1975. He went on this mission to four cities: Los Angeles, New York, Miami, and Panama. That's when he tried to promote our products, but it wasn't successful. Later, I had a high school classmate who was studying in Los Angeles, so he was there. Because of that, my father bought a house in Los Angeles. That's why I came to America. My first time was in 1975.

answer_text: 他家裡有錢, 他可以出來, 因為爸爸沒錢, 要在香港讀大學。 那時候, 在美國, 從香港讀書, 對, 因為當時我朋友說香港最多人去UH讀書 因為他們的選擇是美國最便宜的大學 所以很多人來 現在是否我不知道 當時他說最便宜,為何這麼多人來考驗 另外又近呢 那時候有大拉斐 那時候有新加坡航空機 我回香港前有用大拉斐 有ASR、AMS,但現在不在了 那時候有新加坡航空機 那時候有一個人在文化廣場 我們很熟悉的 其實我爸爸在以前 我爸爸在那時和一間公司合作 叫做Home Beautiful 在香港酒店 在香港酒店購物 跟他商量 他有一個小伙伴在那裡做 大家覺得不適合 我們就自己開了 當我參與了CUHK時 我已經在做生意 跟Home Beautiful 對,Home Beautiful 因為有另外一個人 那時候Culture Plaza是一個台灣群組的 他認識一個台灣人 叫他來Home Beautiful 他在Culture Plaza開了一個傢俬舖 就這樣了 那時候就是1976 那時候就是 1974 oil crisis 整個美國都在逆向 他的生意就開了在1974 那個生意不太好 原本有香港派了一個人來 Send people from Hong Kong Manage for他 那個叫陳生 那個人說那裡No future No business 所以他就回香港 他回香港 他怎樣 他見到我了 他說你有沒有興趣去呀 那時我不知道他在那裡 我說好啦,就來到Home Beautiful 在Culture Plaza 他叫我來時 他說你來,我去拿個Green Card for you 他就來了 來了之後 年紀了 我說為什麼還沒有跟我申請這個Green Card呢 那時那個律師 有時見到那位律師,他說這家Home Beautiful已經有四個人 台灣的Home Beautiful有四個人來申請Green Card 他已經拿了,cannot have no more That's why那時剛剛Vanilla Hotel renovation finish 他有shopping,shopping那裏有地方可以rent out 其他Business,everybody有gift shop,everything 沒有Chinese Furniture Shop 所以我們那時剛剛在Culture Park,我們認識一個人 那個人做Jewelry,他是employee 他認識一個人在Vanilla Hotel,他介紹我去 所以就拿了個舖位 拿了舖位 原本應該是用Home Beautiful來開 因為那個lawyer說不行 因為Home Beautiful已經有四個人拿了那個舖位 所以爸爸用了這個 Krigenwood 在Murray Mall開了 那時他有份,那個人叫我來 但是他後來他不要走了 因為那時是以我們的名字 我們申請Green Card的law是很難的 為什麼呢 原本我們那個quota應該到的 你知道嗎,你申請Green Card來這裡 China有quota,有20,000,全美國給你 但是爸爸那時,我們的quota快到了 那時的quota主要是香港、台灣那些人 那時Jimmy Carter是President 他就認識這個Mainland China 那時是1979年 我們就正式結婚 那時的意思是 在中國來的那些人 每個人都用了所有的quota 所以假設我是1979年 我就應該可以拿到Green Card了 我們要等到83、84才能拿到Green Card
label: Did he learn it?
visitor_question: How did you manage your visa to stay?
question_zh: 你是如何处理签证以便留下来的？
answer_text_en:
# Translation

He had money at home, so he could come out. Because my dad didn't have much money, I had to go to university in Hong Kong. Back then, in America, studying from Hong Kong, right, because at the time my friends said Hong Kong had the most people going to UH to study because their choice was the cheapest university in America, so many people came. Whether that's still the case now, I don't know. Back then they said it was the cheapest, so why did so many people come to try? Plus it was closer too. Back there we had the big flights. Singapore Airlines was around back then. I used the big flights before coming back to Hong Kong. There was ASR, AMS, but they're not around anymore now. Singapore Airlines was there back then. There was a person at Culture Plaza we were very familiar with. Actually my father back in those days, my father at that time partnered with a company called Home Beautiful at the Hong Kong hotel. Shopping at the Hong Kong hotel. We discussed it with them. He had a small partner working there. We all thought it wasn't suitable, so we opened our own. When I got involved with CUHK, I was already doing business with Home Beautiful. Right, Home Beautiful. Because there was another person. Back then Culture Plaza was a Taiwanese group. He knew a Taiwanese person and invited him to Home Beautiful. He opened a furniture shop at Culture Plaza. That's how it went. That was around 1976. Actually it was 1974, the oil crisis. The whole of America was going backwards. His business opened in 1974. That business didn't go so well. Originally Hong Kong sent someone over to manage it. That guy was called Mr. Chan. He said there's no future, no business there, so he went back to Hong Kong. He came back and saw me, and he said, "Are you interested in coming?" At the time I didn't know what he was doing there. I said okay, so I came to Home Beautiful at Culture Plaza. When he called me to come, he said, "Come, I'll get you a Green Card." So he came. But later, as I got older, I said, "Why haven't you applied for the Green Card for me yet?" At that time, that lawyer said that Home Beautiful already had four people. The Taiwan Home Beautiful had four people applying for Green Cards. He already got his, cannot have no more. That's why. Just then the Vanilla Hotel renovation finished. They had shopping, there was space to rent out for other businesses. Everybody had a gift shop, everything. But there was no Chinese furniture shop. So around that time at Culture Park, we knew someone who did jewelry, he was an employee. He knew someone at the Vanilla Hotel and introduced me. So we got a shop space. We got the space. Originally we should have opened it under Home Beautiful, but the lawyer said no, because Home Beautiful already had four people who got the quota for shop spaces. So my father used the name Krigenwood and opened at Murray Mall. Back then he was involved, that person asked me to come, but he didn't want to continue later because it was under our names. Getting a Green Card through us was very difficult legally. Why? Originally our quota was supposed to arrive. You know, when you apply for a Green Card to come here, China has a quota—20,000 for the whole United States. But at that time, our quota was running out. The quota back then was mainly for people coming from Hong Kong and Taiwan. Jimmy Carter was President at the time. He then recognized Mainland China. That was 1979. We formally got married around then. The point was that people coming from China were using up all the quota. So if I was 1979, I should have been able to get a Green Card. But we had to wait until '83, '84 to finally get it.

answer_text_en: L1, L1 is a company transfer. So I had Jergenwood Furniture in Hong Kong. So with this visa, every year I had to apply for a visa. We all knew you had to apply, and back then it was extremely, extremely difficult. Because every year you had to do a TB test. I had to do the TB test myself. I had to handle it on my own because there were too many lawyers involved. So when it comes to Jergenwood Furniture in Hong Kong, the original visa situation was pretty good. At that time I applied for a place called Ocean Center—a new shopping mall. It had offices upstairs and three levels of shopping center below. We applied for that location. That location was really prime. So my dad came here.

label: Why did he make money that way?
visitor_question: How much money could you make on custom furniture?

answer_text_en: Because back then in Hong Kong, there were so many buildings and so many people, lots of new construction everywhere. People were making money hand over fist. So all their furniture—everything was custom made. He said, "I'm spending this much money, that much money"—they didn't even talk about the actual cost anymore, just quoted the asking price. It's the same here with furniture—you can go and get things customized. So take someone like Xia Sheng, right? He made a lot of money. A dining table—they could buy one for 40,000 Hong Kong dollars back then. But it probably only cost him 10,000 to make. But us? Back then, there was this old fellow who complained to me. He said people were buying tables for 12,000, 20,000, and he was saying his cost was only 12,000 Hong Kong dollars.

label: How much was the revenue?
visitor_question: How did disagreements affect the business?
question_zh: 分歧如何影響了業務？
answer_text_en:
I don't really know, but I know them—whatever I say, they oppose it. You see, we're talking about... it's like... no personal feelings. What we do, design anything, we just say, according to what the customer likes. Service your customer. Come on.

You know, like back when my father was around, didn't I tell you? At that time I didn't have a Green Card, so for about six or seven years, I didn't go back to Hong Kong. I didn't go back. But the first thing I did when I got back to Hong Kong was to go everywhere, do a survey, study how much is selling, what's not selling—what makes the business better.

Back then, in one month a business could make... I heard from suppliers, some people doing furniture could make 3 million. We maybe made 200,000 or 300,000 at most. That's the difference.

At that time I was gathering statistics. I told Uncle Bill—Uncle Bill heard—I said to him, don't deceive yourself. You know why? Don't just copy. Don't worry about your competitor. The problem with him now is he has an inferiority complex. He can't conquer it. If you say someone else is good, then you feel low, you put yourself down.

But for me, the hard figures mean we can do better, you understand?

He didn't pass the high school exam. He didn't finish high school either. Went straight to Dragonwood after that. Your sister also didn't finish high school. Alex was too young, didn't finish either. If you don't pass the high school exam, you can't go to college. But a lot of people didn't pass back then—forty to forty-five percent didn't pass. They had a curve, so it was actually unfair, but that's how it was.

Dragonwood lasted from '76 to '87, about eleven years. At that time they took back everything—we had to renovate all the shops. It wasn't worth it to find a new location.

We took a business, very humble. We supplied from our own factory, but they never delivered on schedule. They ordered furniture, but the factory didn't manufacture properly. Two months later, somebody would complain. It was a mess.

Back in Hong Kong, we had good people like Mr. Xia. Mr. Du was very good—whenever there was an order, he'd get the furniture done right. But these other small factories, each doing their own thing... He would check progress every week, but they never checked. They just kept going.

So sometimes when we went back to Hong Kong, in the Ocean Centre tool shop—they bought the most paper towels, and that was good profit. Every weekend when so many people came, they'd pull out a sample to show, but they'd make a mess of everything and then people couldn't even buy things. I've said this so many times already—why is this still the best?

visitor_question: Tell us about your first big Japanese client.

answer_text_en:
So back then, we had just started this Jergenwood Furniture on Kenton Road, not long after I married your mom. We started this furniture business, and we had a Japanese client who saw our work. There was a Japanese company—the name was on Kenton Road—they were buying very expensive pieces. He said around three or four thousand Hong Kong dollars, very pricey stuff. He gave us our first order for 100,000. Then the second order, there were two orders actually, and the other one was also 100,000. So after the first order came through, the second order followed. Back then, you understand, 100,000 was the price of an apartment. We had done work on apartments in the Kowloon area for that same price—100,000.

But then that person, after he did that first order, the second order got cancelled. By that time though, we had already finished it. So we wondered why—because he was Japanese, I went with Uncle Bill to investigate. Uncle Bill, he was from Fukuoka. We went to Japan to check out his factory. He showed us... "No, no," he said, "look, look here." It was because of those Keto Company pieces, you see, the painting—the varnish was most important, most important. But what they were doing, they were applying very little paint.
